text: The surgeon places ports in the left upper quadrant, aligning them in a straight line with eight centimeters spacing towards the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to suspend the transverse colon and right colon to the abdominal wall, enhancing exposure for dissection.
The surgeon opens the mesenteric envelope and dissects the tumors using a medial-lateral approach. The duodenum is exposed, and the ileocolic vessels are identified.
The surgeon clips and divides the ileocolic vein originating from the superior mesenteric vein. The distal ileum is identified, and the mesentery is divided to address adhesions in the pelvis.
The surgeon frees the small intestine, preparing it for anastomosis later in the operation.
With the robotic fourth arm providing proper exposure, the surgeon continues the cephalad dissection and identifies the right colic vein, which is clipped and divided using robotic clips.
The surgeon identifies the right colic artery, clips it, and divides it, following the previous dissection of the right colic vein.
The surgical field shows proximity to the pancreas and duodenum, with these structures clearly visible in the operative area.
The surgeon continues dissection on top of the pancreas and duodenum, carefully separating tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to take down the suture, allowing for further retraction and improved exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the right branch of the midcolic vessels, clips them, and divides them using surgical instruments.

The surgical team replaces a sponge underneath the transverse colon, pulling it caudally to enter the lesser sac. They divide the omentum and identify the right gastroepiploic vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach, which are then divided. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon attempts to identify and connect the spaces from the opposite side, where the sponge was previously left, revealing the gastrocolic trunk.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the hepatic flexure to mobilize the right colon, using the previously taken down suture to relieve tension during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the distal ileum using a surgical instrument, completing the separation of this section of the small intestine.
The surgeon marks the transverse colon with a suture and proceeds to divide it, following previous dissection steps.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to completely divide any remaining attachments around the hepatic flexure, ensuring full mobilization of the right colon.
The surgeon has completed the disconnection of the anatomical structures, visually confirming the separation of the previously connected areas.
The surgeon places the specimen on the side after completing the dissection and mobilization of the right colon.
The distal ileum is positioned adjacent to the transverse colon, showing the spatial relationship between these two sections of the intestine.
The surgeon parks the anastomosis in the right upper quadrant, placing a suture and using the fourth arm of the robotic system for optimal exposure.
The surgeon demonstrates an isoperistaltic anastomosis, showing the alignment of the bowel segments with matching peristaltic directions.
The surgeon uses ICG to assess the blood supply of the small intestine and transverse colon, followed by the use of a 45mm stapler to perform the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to align the anastomosis between the distal ileum and the transverse colon in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon closes the common channel opening using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture, employing a canal suture technique with a nine-inch length.
The completed anastomosis is visible, with the barbed suture locked on itself to secure the connection.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to assess the blood supply of the anastomosis, ensuring adequate perfusion to the connected intestinal segments.
The surgeon removes all sutures from the completed anastomosis site, revealing the underlying tissue.
The specimen is extracted through the stapler port, with four visible incisions on the patient's abdomen.
